tier_2_handbook.definition: Thyroid carcinoma is a malignancy arising from the follicular cells or parafollicular C cells of the thyroid gland. The thyroid gland is a bi-lobed endocrine organ located in the anterior neck, overlying the trachea and larynx, and is derived embryologically from the floor of the pharynx (thyroglossal duct). The primary function of thyroid follicular cells is the synthesis, storage, and secretion of thyroid hormones, while parafollicular C cells produce calcitonin. Thyroid carcinomas are classified based on their cell of origin, histological features, and molecular alterations. The main types include differentiated thyroid carcinomas (papillary, follicular, and oncocytic), which originate from follicular cells and often retain the ability to concentrate iodine; medullary thyroid carcinoma, arising from neuroendocrine C cells; and anaplastic (undifferentiated) thyroid carcinoma, a highly aggressive tumor that may arise from dedifferentiation of a pre-existing differentiated carcinoma [ST-1, MEDU-1, ANAP-1].

subtypes.name: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma (PTC)
subtypes.description: The most common histotype of thyroid carcinoma, characterized by a papillary growth pattern and distinctive nuclear features (Orphan Annie eye nuclei, nuclear grooves, intranuclear pseudoinclusions). It often presents as a solitary thyroid nodule and has a propensity for lymph node metastasis. Several histologic variants exist, including follicular variant, tall cell, columnar cell, hobnail, diffuse sclerosing, and solid/trabecular variants, some of which are associated with more aggressive behavior [PAP-1, PAP-4, Discussion].
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 89.8% of all thyroid carcinoma diagnoses (2011-2015 data) [Discussion, MS-2].
subtypes.key_associations: Often associated with BRAF V600E mutation (~45%), RET/PTC rearrangements, RAS mutations, and PAX8/PPARγ rearrangements. Familial syndromes include familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) with cribriform-morular variant and Cowden syndrome (PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome) [THYR-E, Discussion].

subtypes.name: Follicular Thyroid Carcinoma (FTC)
subtypes.description: A differentiated thyroid carcinoma characterized by a follicular growth pattern. The diagnosis requires histologic evidence of capsular or vascular invasion, which cannot be determined by FNA. It is classified as minimally invasive (encapsulated with microscopic capsular invasion, no vascular invasion) or widely invasive. Follicular carcinomas typically metastasize via the bloodstream (hematogenous spread) rather than lymphatics [FOLL-1, Discussion].
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 4.5% of thyroid carcinoma diagnoses (2011-2015 data) [Discussion, MS-2].
subtypes.key_associations: Molecular alterations often involve the RAS/RAF/MAPK pathway. PAX8/PPARγ rearrangements and RAS mutations are common. It is associated with iodine deficiency and radiation exposure. Familial syndromes include Cowden syndrome (PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome) [THYR-E, Discussion].

subtypes.name: Oncocytic Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Formerly known as Hürthle cell carcinoma, this is a thyroid carcinoma composed predominantly (≥75%) of oncocytic (oxyphilic) cells with abundant, granular, eosinophilic cytoplasm rich in mitochondria. Like FTC, the diagnosis requires demonstration of capsular or vascular invasion. It was reclassified as a separate entity from FTC by WHO and AJCC in 2017. It tends to be more aggressive and is less likely to concentrate iodine compared to other differentiated thyroid cancers [ONC-1, Discussion].
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 1.8% of thyroid carcinoma diagnoses (2011-2015 data) [Discussion, MS-2].
subtypes.key_associations: Molecular alterations include mutations in the RAS/RAF/MAPK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathways, as well as EIF1AX, TERT, DAXX genes, mitochondrial DNA variations, and copy number alterations. Lymph node metastases are more common than in FTC [Discussion, MS-29].

subtypes.name: Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma (MTC)
subtypes.description: A neuroendocrine tumor arising from the parafollicular C cells of the thyroid, which produce calcitonin. It does not concentrate iodine. It can be sporadic (80% of cases) or hereditary (20%) due to germline pathogenic variants (PVs) in the RET proto-oncogene. Hereditary forms include Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2A (MEN2A), MEN2B, and familial MTC. It often presents with an elevated serum calcitonin level and may be associated with diarrhea and flushing due to hormone secretion [MEDU-1, MEDU-3, Discussion].
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 1.6% of thyroid carcinoma diagnoses (2011-2015 data) [Discussion, MS-2].
subtypes.key_associations: Germline RET PVs (in >95% of MEN2A) and somatic RET mutations (in ≥25% of sporadic cases). MEN2A is associated with pheochromocytoma and hyperparathyroidism. MEN2B is associated with pheochromocytoma and mucosal neuromas/marfanoid habitus [MEDU-1, MEDU-3, THYR-E].

subtypes.name: Anaplastic Thyroid Carcinoma (ATC)
subtypes.description: An aggressive, undifferentiated thyroid malignancy. It often arises from pre-existing differentiated thyroid carcinoma through dedifferentiation. It is characterized by rapid growth, local invasion into neck structures, and early distant metastases. It does not concentrate iodine and lacks TSH receptors [ANAP-1, Discussion].
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 0.8% of thyroid carcinoma diagnoses (2011-2015 data) [Discussion, MS-2].
subtypes.key_associations: Molecular alterations include TP53 mutations (loss of p53), BRAF V600E mutations (~20-40%), TERT promoter mutations, and alterations in the PI3K/AKT/mTOR and RAS/RAF/MAPK pathways. Approximately 50% of cases arise from or coexist with a differentiated thyroid carcinoma [ANAP-1, THYR-B 3/5, Discussion].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of thyroid carcinomas varies by histotype. Differentiated thyroid carcinomas (PTC, FTC, oncocytic) are characterized by alterations in the RAS/RAF/MAPK signaling pathway. BRAF V600E is the most common driver mutation in PTC, occurring in about 45% of cases; it is associated with poorer prognosis when concurrent with TERT promoter mutations. RET/PTC rearrangements and RAS mutations are also frequent in PTC. Follicular and oncocytic carcinomas commonly harbor RAS mutations, PAX8/PPARγ rearrangements, and, in the case of oncocytic carcinoma, unique mitochondrial DNA variations and copy number alterations. NIFTP, formerly a subtype of PTC, is characterized by RAS, PAX8/PPARγ, and/or BRAF mutations (excluding BRAF V600E) [Discussion, MS-5, MS-9, MS-10, MS-16, MS-53]. Medullary thyroid carcinoma is driven by mutations in the RET proto-oncogene. Germline RET PVs cause hereditary forms (MEN2A, MEN2B, familial MTC). Somatic RET mutations (e.g., in codons 11, 13, 16) are found in at least 25% of sporadic MTC tumors and are associated with poorer prognosis [MEDU-1, MEDU-3, Discussion, MS-30, MS-31]. Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma is characterized by loss of differentiation and multiple genetic alterations, including TP53 mutations, TERT promoter mutations, and, in a subset, BRAF V600E mutations (~20-40%). Approximately 50% of ATCs arise from or are coexistent with a differentiated thyroid carcinoma [ANAP-1, THYR-B 3/5, Discussion, MS-37, MS-51].

epidemiology.incidence: For the U.S. population, the lifetime risk of being diagnosed with thyroid carcinoma is 1.2%. It is estimated that approximately 44,020 new cases of thyroid carcinoma will be diagnosed in the United States in 2024. Thyroid carcinoma is currently the eighth most common malignancy diagnosed in individuals assigned female at birth (AFAB). A cohort study of data collected by the International Agency for Research on Cancer from 1998 to 2012 showed that the global incidence of papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) increased during this time [Discussion, MS-2, MS-8]. The estimated incidence of thyroid carcinoma previously increased by an average of ~5% annually between 2004 and 2013, but the incidence rate has since stabilized, likely due to more conservative indications for thyroid biopsy and the reclassification of NIFTP [Discussion, MS-2].
epidemiology.mortality: In 2024, it is estimated that approximately 2170 cancer deaths will occur among persons with thyroid carcinoma in the United States. Mortality rates are generally very low, with differentiated thyroid carcinomas having 10-year survival rates exceeding 90% to 95%. Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma is almost uniformly lethal. The overall mortality rate has not dramatically increased since 1975 (1150 vs. 2060 deaths) [Discussion, MS-2].
epidemiology.trends: The incidence of thyroid carcinoma has increased, but the increase may reflect earlier detection of subclinical disease (small papillary carcinomas). There is no evidence of improvement in disease-specific survival for patients with distantly metastatic differentiated thyroid cancer. Survival rates for anaplastic thyroid carcinoma may be increasing in recent years [Discussion, MS-2, MS-7, MS-37].
epidemiology.demographics: Thyroid nodules and carcinoma are approximately four times more common in individuals AFAB than in individuals assigned male at birth (AMAB). The disease is also diagnosed more often in white North Americans than in African Americans. Age is an important prognostic variable, with age ≥55 years being a key factor in the AJCC 8th edition staging for differentiated thyroid cancer. The mean age at diagnosis for anaplastic thyroid carcinoma is approximately 71 years, with fewer than 10% of patients being <50 years old [Discussion, MS-2, MS-7, MS-8, MS-12, MS-37].

risk_factors.detail: Exposure to ionizing radiation, particularly in childhood, is the only known environmental cause of thyroid carcinoma and usually causes papillary carcinoma. The thyroid glands of children are especially vulnerable, with risk at about 0.10 Gy. New nodules develop at a rate of about 2% annually after exposure to head and neck irradiation, reaching a peak incidence within 30 years of irradiation. Radiation-induced papillary carcinoma tends to appear more aggressive histologically [Discussion, MS-3, MS-5].

A family history of thyroid carcinoma is indicative of malignancy. Familial non-medullary carcinoma accounts for about 5% of PTCs. For familial papillary carcinoma, most studies require at least three first-degree relatives to be diagnosed. Microscopic familial papillary carcinoma tends to be multifocal and bilateral, often with vascular invasion and high recurrence rates [THYR-E, Discussion, MS-8].

Several inherited cancer syndromes are associated with an increased risk of thyroid carcinoma. These include: Cowden syndrome (PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome, associated with follicular thyroid cancer), familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP, associated with cribriform-morular variant of PTC), Carney complex, and multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 (MEN2A and MEN2B, associated with medullary thyroid carcinoma) [THYR-E, Discussion, MS-8].
Iodine deficiency is associated with follicular carcinoma and anaplastic carcinomas. It may also increase the risk for radiation-induced thyroid cancer [Discussion, MS-3].

Thyroid carcinoma occurs two to three times more often in individuals AFAB than in individuals AMAB. However, thyroid cancer is more aggressive in individuals AMAB, with a risk of death about twice as high [Discussion, MS-2, MS-7, MS-8].

Age is the most important prognostic variable for thyroid cancer mortality. The AJCC 8th edition staging uses an age cutoff of 55 years to predict the presence of distant metastasis [Discussion, MS-7, MS-12].
Palpable thyroid nodules are present in about 5% of the U.S. population for individuals ≥50 years. New nodules develop at a rate of about 0.1% per year beginning in early life [Discussion, MS-2].

protective_factors: Young age at diagnosis (for differentiated carcinomas)
Early stage at diagnosis (tumor confined to thyroid)

management_principles.overview: Thyroid carcinoma management is guided by histologic subtype, tumor stage, and patient factors. The primary treatment is surgery, followed by radioactive iodine (RAI) ablation in selected patients and thyroid hormone therapy. Management is multidisciplinary, involving endocrinology, surgery, oncology, and radiology. The goal is to maximize cure rates while minimizing morbidity, considering the typically excellent prognosis of differentiated thyroid carcinomas (10-year survival >90%) and the poor prognosis of anaplastic carcinoma (median survival ~5 months). [1, 8, 11, 522, 524]

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with localized or locoregional disease (e.g., papillary, follicular, oncocytic, medullary carcinomas).
by_intent.approach: Surgical resection (total thyroidectomy or lobectomy) is the primary curative treatment. For differentiated carcinomas, RAI ablation may be used to eliminate residual disease or as adjuvant therapy. TSH suppression with levothyroxine is standard. For medullary carcinoma, total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection is indicated. [3, 375, 430, 431]

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with advanced, recurrent, or metastatic disease not amenable to curative treatment (e.g., RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer, advanced medullary carcinoma, anaplastic carcinoma).
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy (e.g., tyrosine kinase inhibitors, immunotherapy), external beam radiation therapy (EBRT), and supportive care. Clinical trials are encouraged. [11, 322, 328, 430, 431, 520]

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with low-risk papillary thyroid microcarcinoma (≤1 cm) without aggressive features.
by_intent.approach: Active surveillance with neck ultrasound every 6 months for 1–2 years, then annually, with consideration for surgery if growth or new features develop. [3, 380–384, THYR-D]

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary team (MDT) consultation is required for all thyroid cancer patients, especially for anaplastic carcinoma, advanced or recurrent disease, and complex cases. The MDT should include endocrinologists, surgeons, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, and radiologists. For anaplastic carcinoma, expedient MDT consultation is critical. [ANAP-1, ANAP-2, 520]
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status influences treatment intensity. Kinase inhibitor therapy may not be appropriate for patients with stable or slowly progressive indolent disease. For anaplastic carcinoma, treatment decisions must consider the patient's overall condition and goals of care. [THYR-B, 520]

management_pathways.title: Low-Risk Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma
branching_basis: Tumor size ≤4 cm, confined to thyroid, no extrathyroidal extension (ETE), no lymph node metastases (N0/NX), no distant metastases (M0), favorable histology.

nodes.criteria: Tumor ≤4 cm, unifocal or multifocal (all foci ≤1 cm), no ETE, no lymph node metastases, postoperative unstimulated thyroglobulin (Tg) <1 ng/mL, negative postoperative ultrasound.

options.label: Lobectomy (preferred) or total thyroidectomy (category 2B).
options.preference: Preferred: Lobectomy. Total thyroidectomy may be considered for bilateral nodularity or patient preference.

options.label: Not typically recommended.
options.preference: Not recommended if all low-risk criteria are present.

adjuvant_logic.summary: RAI ablation is not routinely recommended for very low-risk disease.

management_pathways.title: High-Risk Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma
branching_basis: Tumor >4 cm, ETE, lymph node metastases (N1), distant metastases (M1), aggressive histology (tall cell, columnar cell, hobnail, diffuse sclerosing, solid/trabecular), differentiated high-grade carcinoma.

nodes.criteria: Any high-risk feature (e.g., T3/T4, N1, M1, aggressive histology).

options.label: Total thyroidectomy with therapeutic neck dissection for clinically apparent/biopsy-proven disease.
Typically recommended for remnant ablation or adjuvant therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: RAI is recommended based on clinicopathologic risk factors (PAP-4). Dose depends on risk (30–50 mCi for ablation, 75–150 mCi for adjuvant, 100–200 mCi for known disease). [THYR-C]

management_pathways.title: Follicular and Oncocytic Carcinoma
branching_basis: Histology (follicular or oncocytic), invasion pattern (capsular vs. vascular), tumor size, lymph node involvement (less common in follicular).

nodes.criteria: Follicular neoplasm on FNA (Bethesda IV), clinical suspicion of malignancy.

options.label: Lobectomy/isthmusectomy for diagnostic/therapeutic purposes. Total thyroidectomy if bilateral nodularity or tumor >4 cm.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Surgical pathology determines extent (minimally invasive vs. invasive). Completion thyroidectomy may be needed for invasive cancer (≥4 vessels) or other high-risk features. [FOLL-1, ONC-1]

nodes.criteria: Confirmed invasive follicular/oncocytic carcinoma (e.g., extensive vascular invasion, gross ETE).

options.label: Total thyroidectomy with therapeutic neck dissection for involved compartments.
Selective recommendation based on clinicopathologic factors (e.g., large tumor, vascular invasion, N1, Tg levels).

adjuvant_logic.summary: RAI decisions are based on clinicopathologic factors (FOLL-3, ONC-3). Oncocytic carcinoma is often non-iodine-avid, so FDG-PET may be considered. [FOLL-9, ONC-9]

management_pathways.title: Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma
branching_basis: Sporadic vs. hereditary (germline RET PV), RET mutation risk level, presence of pheochromocytoma/hyperparathyroidism.

options.label: Total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection (level VI). Therapeutic lateral neck dissection for clinically involved nodes.
For unresectable, recurrent, or metastatic disease.
options.preference: Preferred: Selpercatinib for RET-mutant disease; vandetanib or cabozantinib for others.
options.recommendation: Category 1 for vandetanib, cabozantinib, and selpercatinib in RET-mutant disease.

adjuvant_logic.summary: No role for RAI. Postoperative levothyroxine to normalize TSH (not suppress). [MEDU-1, THYR-B]

nodes.criteria: Hereditary MTC with germline RET PV (MEN2A/MEN2B).

options.label: Prophylactic total thyroidectomy based on RET mutation risk level and age.
options.preference: Highest-risk (M918T): surgery during first year of life. High-risk (codon 634, A883F): surgery before age 5. Moderate/low-risk: may delay if surveillance is normal.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Screen for pheochromocytoma and hyperparathyroidism preoperatively. Genetic counseling for family members. [MEDU-3, MEDU-4]

management_pathways.title: Anaplastic Thyroid Carcinoma
branching_basis: Resectable vs. unresectable locoregional disease, presence of distant metastases, BRAF V600E mutation status.

options.label: Total thyroidectomy with therapeutic lymph node dissection if R0/R1 resection achievable.
For BRAF V600E-mutated disease, neoadjuvant dabrafenib/trametinib may be considered (category 2B).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant EBRT with radiosensitizing chemotherapy (e.g., carboplatin/paclitaxel) is recommended when clinically appropriate. [ANAP-2, THYR-B]

options.label: Targeted therapy based on molecular testing (BRAF, NTRK, RET, ALK). For BRAF V600E, dabrafenib/trametinib or binimetinib/encorafenib. For others, consider clinical trials, immunotherapy (ipilimumab/nivolumab), or chemotherapy.
options.preference: Preferred: Dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E; other targeted agents for specific fusions.

options.label: Best supportive care, including airway management and palliative RT.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Prophylactic tracheostomy should be avoided. Early palliative care discussion is essential. [ANAP-3, THYR-B]

management_pathways.title: RAI-Refractory Differentiated Thyroid Cancer (Papillary, Follicular, Oncocytic)
branching_basis: Structural disease progression despite RAI, lack of RAI uptake, or RAI-avid but progressive disease.

nodes.criteria: RAI-refractory disease with progressive, symptomatic, or threatening locoregional or distant metastases.

options.label: Preferred: Lenvatinib (category 1) or sorafenib (category 1). For BRAF V600E-mutant, consider dabrafenib/trametinib. For NTRK/RET fusions, specific inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib; selpercatinib, pralsetinib). For MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H, pembrolizumab.
options.preference: Lenvatinib preferred over sorafenib based on higher ORR (65% vs. 12% in SELECT trial).
options.recommendation: Category 1 for lenvatinib, sorafenib, dabrafenib/trametinib (BRAF+), selpercatinib (RET+).

adjuvant_logic.summary: TSH should remain suppressed. Brain imaging prior to systemic therapy due to bleeding risk with TKIs. Consider local therapies (EBRT, surgery, ablation) for oligometastatic disease. [PAP-10, FOLL-9, ONC-9, THYR-B]

items.text: Thyroid and neck ultrasound (including central and lateral cervical nodal compartments) for all patients with suspected or known thyroid cancer.
Consider neck CT/MRI with contrast when suspicious nodes are detected by ultrasound and/or for vocal cord paresis.
FDG-PET/CT or FDG-PET/MRI for anaplastic carcinoma to assess extent and metastases.
CT with contrast of head, neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis for anaplastic carcinoma.
Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT for medullary carcinoma if calcitonin >300 pg/mL or for imaging of neuroendocrine metastases.
FNA of thyroid nodule and suspicious lymph nodes. Tg washout useful if cytology negative.
Molecular analysis (BRAF, NTRK, ALK, RET, MSI, dMMR, TMB) for advanced, progressive, or threatening disease to guide systemic therapy.
Germline RET PV testing for all medullary thyroid carcinoma patients.
TSH, Tg, TgAb for differentiated thyroid carcinoma.
Basal serum calcitonin and CEA for medullary carcinoma.
Screen for pheochromocytoma (plasma/urine fractionated metanephrines and catecholamines) in medullary carcinoma.
Serum calcium and parathyroid hormone for medullary carcinoma with suspected hyperparathyroidism.
Vocal cord mobility assessment (ultrasound, laryngoscopy) for abnormal voice, invasive disease, or bulky central neck disease.
Bronchoscopy for anaplastic carcinoma with airway invasion.
Multidisciplinary team consultation for all thyroid cancer, especially anaplastic carcinoma.
Genetic counseling for patients with hereditary syndromes (MEN2, Cowden syndrome, FAP, DICER1).

surgery.role: Primary curative treatment for all thyroid carcinoma types. Also used for recurrent or persistent disease.

procedures.name: Total Thyroidectomy
procedures.approach: Surgical removal of the entire thyroid gland with or without central neck dissection.
procedures.indications: Papillary carcinoma with T3/T4, N1, M1, aggressive histology, prior radiation, bilateral nodularity, or tumor >4 cm. Medullary carcinoma (all cases). Anaplastic carcinoma if resectable. Follicular/oncocytic carcinoma with invasive features (≥4 vessels, gross ETE). [PAP-1, FOLL-1, ONC-1, MEDU-1, ANAP-2]
procedures.key_complications: Hypoparathyroidism (transient 5.4%, permanent 1.1%), recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (transient 3.4%, permanent 3.4%), superior laryngeal nerve injury. [228, 229, 230]
procedures.operative_mortality: Low; not specified in source but generally <1%.

procedures.name: Lobectomy/Isthmusectomy
procedures.approach: Removal of one thyroid lobe and isthmus.
procedures.indications: Low-risk papillary carcinoma ≤4 cm without high-risk features. Follicular/oncocytic neoplasm without evidence of invasion. Preferred for patients who cannot tolerate bilateral surgery or refuse lifelong thyroid hormone. [PAP-1, FOLL-1, ONC-1]
procedures.key_complications: Similar to total thyroidectomy but unilateral hypoparathyroidism possible. Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury risk lower.

procedures.name: Therapeutic Neck Dissection
procedures.approach: Formal compartmental resection of involved nodal basins (central level VI, lateral levels II–V).
procedures.indications: Clinically apparent or biopsy-proven nodal metastases. Not recommended as prophylaxis for papillary carcinoma with cN0. [PAP-1, MEDU-1]
procedures.key_complications: Injury to spinal accessory nerve, phrenic nerve, or jugular vein. Chylorrhea.

procedures.name: Prophylactic Thyroidectomy
procedures.approach: Total thyroidectomy in children/infants with RET PV mutations.
procedures.indications: Hereditary medullary carcinoma (MEN2A/MEN2B) based on RET mutation risk. Timing varies: highest-risk (M918T) during first year of life; high-risk (codon 634, A883F) before age 5. [MEDU-3, MEDU-4]
procedures.key_complications: Same as total thyroidectomy; higher risk in very young children.

surgery.resectability_criteria: For anaplastic carcinoma, resectability depends on extent of involved structures, potential morbidity, and mortality. R0/R1 resection is goal. In most cases, debulking surgery is not indicated. For differentiated thyroid carcinomas, completeness of resection is assessed by margins and invasion. [ANAP-2, ST-1]
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Complete workup including imaging, molecular testing, and multidisciplinary consultation. For medullary carcinoma, pheochromocytoma must be excluded before surgery. For anaplastic carcinoma, expedient evaluation and consideration of neoadjuvant therapy if borderline resectable. [MEDU-1, ANAP-1, ANAP-2]

radiation_therapy.role: Adjunct to surgery for locoregional control, palliation of symptoms, and treatment of unresectable disease.
principles: EBRT is considered for gross residual disease after surgery, unresectable locoregional recurrence, and palliation of symptomatic metastases.
IMRT technique is encouraged to reduce toxicity.
For anaplastic carcinoma, EBRT should start as quickly as possible; for R0/R1 resection, adjuvant EBRT should begin 2–3 weeks postoperatively.
Concurrent chemoradiation may be considered for anaplastic carcinoma.
EBRT is not routinely recommended for differentiated thyroid carcinoma if RAI is effective and no gross residual disease.

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant EBRT for Differentiated/Medullary Carcinoma (R1 resection)
dose_frameworks.indication: Microscopic residual disease (thyroid bed, involved lymph nodes). Elective nodal regions 50–56 Gy. [THYR-C]

dose_frameworks.name: Salvage EBRT for Differentiated/Medullary Carcinoma (R2 resection or inoperable)
Adjuvant EBRT for Anaplastic Carcinoma (R0/R1)
dose_frameworks.indication: Microscopic disease/high-risk regions. Elective nodal regions can be treated with SIB: 45–54 Gy. [THYR-C]

dose_frameworks.name: Salvage EBRT for Anaplastic Carcinoma (R2 or inoperable)
Palliative EBRT for Metastases
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 8 Gy in 1 fraction; 20 Gy in 5 fractions; 30 Gy in 10 fractions
dose_frameworks.indication: Symptomatic bone or soft tissue metastases. Higher doses (45–60 Gy) for oligometastatic disease with good performance status. [THYR-C]

approaches.name: Concurrent Chemoradiation for Anaplastic Carcinoma
approaches.dose_fractionation: EBRT as above with concurrent radiosensitizing chemotherapy (e.g., weekly paclitaxel, carboplatin/paclitaxel).
approaches.indication: Unresectable or incompletely resected anaplastic carcinoma.
approaches.key_trial: Phase 2 studies show improved local control and survival. [539, 540]

approaches.name: Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS) for Brain Metastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: Typically 18–24 Gy in 1 fraction for solitary lesions.
approaches.indication: Solitary or limited CNS metastases from any thyroid carcinoma type.
approaches.key_trial: NCCN Guidelines for Central Nervous System Cancers.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used for recurrent, persistent, or metastatic thyroid carcinoma not amenable to surgery or RAI. Kinase inhibitors and targeted therapies have improved outcomes. Traditional cytotoxic chemotherapy has minimal efficacy in differentiated thyroid cancer. [11, 311, THYR-B]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: RAI-Refractory Differentiated Thyroid Carcinoma (Papillary, Follicular, Oncocytic) with Progressive/Symptomatic Disease
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Lenvatinib (category 1) or sorafenib (category 1). For BRAF V600E-mutant, consider dabrafenib/trametinib. For NTRK fusion-positive, larotrectinib, entrectinib, or repotrectinib. For RET fusion-positive, selpercatinib or pralsetinib. For MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb), pembrolizumab.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase 3 trials show improved PFS (lenvatinib: 18.3 vs. 3.6 months; sorafenib: 10.8 vs. 5.8 months; cabozantinib: not reached vs. 1.9 months). [322, 328, 361]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cabozantinib after progression on lenvatinib/sorafenib (category 1 for papillary, 2A for follicular/oncocytic). Ipilimumab/nivolumab (oncocytic only). Carboplatin/pemetrexed. Clinical trials. [THYR-B]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Molecular testing for actionable alterations (ALK, NTRK, BRAF, RET fusions; dMMR, MSI, TMB) recommended. Testing preferably on tumor tissue; ctDNA if tumor not available. [PAP-10, FOLL-9, ONC-9]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: For RET-mutant disease: selpercatinib (category 1) or pralsetinib (category 2B). For all others: vandetanib (category 1) or cabozantinib (category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Selpercatinib improved PFS vs. cabozantinib/vandetanib (median not reached vs. 16.8 months; HR 0.28). Vandetanib and cabozantinib improved PFS in phase 3 trials (HR 0.46 and 0.28, respectively). [481, 485, 491]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Lutetium DOTATATE (somatostatin receptor-positive). Dacarbazine-based chemotherapy. Other small-molecule kinase inhibitors (sorafenib, sunitinib, lenvatinib, pazopanib) if trials or preferred options not available. [THYR-B]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Germline RET PV testing for all patients. Somatic RET testing for germline wild-type/unknown. [MEDU-6, MEDU-7]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Anaplastic Thyroid Carcinoma, Metastatic or Unresectable Locoregional
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: For BRAF V600E-mutated tumors: dabrafenib/trametinib or binimetinib/encorafenib. For RET fusion-positive: pralsetinib or selpercatinib. For NTRK fusion-positive: entrectinib, larotrectinib, repotrectinib. For non-RAS tumors: ipilimumab/nivolumab.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Dabrafenib/trametinib showed ORR 56% in BRAF V600E-mutated ATC. Ipilimumab/nivolumab based on phase 2 data in aggressive thyroid carcinoma. [541, 547, 548]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Lenvatinib/pembrolizumab. Carboplatin/pemetrexed after prior treatment. Cisplatin/doxorubicin. Paclitaxel. Clinical trials. [THYR-B]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Biomarker testing should include BRAF, NTRK1/2/3, ALK, and RET. BRAF IHC recommended due to faster turnaround. Testing on tumor tissue preferred. [ANAP-1]

key_regimens.name: Lenvatinib
key_regimens.cycle_days: Continued until progression or unacceptable toxicity.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension (68%), diarrhea (59%), palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (32%), weight loss (31%), decreased appetite (31%). Serious treatment-related adverse events 16%. [322]

key_regimens.name: Sorafenib
key_regimens.cycle_days: Continued until progression or unacceptable toxicity.

key_regimens.name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib
key_regimens.setting: BRAF V600E-mutated RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid carcinoma or anaplastic carcinoma.
key_regimens.cycle_days: Continued until progression or unacceptable toxicity.

key_regimens.name: Selpercatinib
key_regimens.setting: RET-mutant medullary thyroid carcinoma or RET fusion-positive thyroid carcinoma.
key_regimens.cycle_days: Continued until progression or unacceptable toxicity.

key_regimens.name: Vandetanib
key_regimens.cycle_days: Continued until progression or unacceptable toxicity.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Diarrhea (56%), rash (45%), nausea (33%), hypertension (32%). QTc prolongation risk; requires REMS program. [481]

key_regimens.name: Cabozantinib
key_regimens.setting: Progressive, metastatic medullary thyroid carcinoma or RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid carcinoma after lenvatinib/sorafenib.
key_regimens.cycle_days: Continued until progression or unacceptable toxicity.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Diarrhea (63%), palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (50%), weight loss (48%), decreased appetite (46%). Serious hemorrhage or GI perforation rare. [361, 485]

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) advanced thyroid carcinoma.

drugs.name: pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV every 3 weeks or 400 mg IV every 6 weeks

key_regimens.cycle_days: Up to 2 years or until progression/unacceptable toxicity.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated adverse events (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies). [352, 353]

treatment_response_assessment.title: Postoperative Assessment and Surveillance
treatment_response_assessment.timing: First assessment 6–12 weeks after surgery. Subsequent surveillance based on risk stratification.

response_logic.scenario: No evidence of disease (NED) after total thyroidectomy with or without RAI.
response_logic.assessment: Physical examination, TSH, Tg, TgAb measurement. Neck ultrasound at 6–12 months, then every 1–3 years for 5–8 years then discontinue for low-risk patients. For high-risk, consider stimulated Tg and diagnostic RAI imaging. [PAP-7, PAP-8, FOLL-6, FOLL-7, ONC-6, ONC-7]

response_logic.scenario: Biochemical abnormality (rising Tg or new TgAb) without structural disease.
response_logic.assessment: Consider additional imaging (neck CT/MRI, chest CT, FDG-PET, RAI imaging). If no structural disease, continue surveillance. Tg trending not recommended after lobectomy. [PAP-7, FOLL-6, ONC-6]
Biopsy suspicious areas (consider Tg washout). If confirmed recurrence, treat accordingly (surgery, RAI, EBRT, systemic therapy). [PAP-9, PAP-10, FOLL-8, FOLL-9, ONC-8, ONC-9]

Serum calcitonin and CEA every 6–12 months. Neck ultrasound if detectable calcitonin or elevated CEA. If calcitonin ≥150 pg/mL, CT/MRI of neck, chest, liver. Consider Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT or whole-body MRI for very elevated calcitonin. [MEDU-5]

imaging_recommendations: Neck ultrasound: primary modality for locoregional surveillance. [PAP-7, PAP-8, FOLL-6, FOLL-7, ONC-6, ONC-7]
CT or MRI of neck/chest: for suspected advanced disease or to guide surgery.
FDG-PET/CT: for suspected distant metastases when RAI imaging negative.
RAI whole-body scan: considered in high-risk patients or if rising Tg. Post-therapy scan recommended after RAI treatment. [PAP-5, PAP-6, FOLL-5, ONC-5]
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy of suspicious lymph nodes or recurrent lesions before reoperation. Tg washout recommended if cytology negative. [PAP-9, FOLL-8, ONC-8]
Surgical resection is preferred for resectable locoregional recurrence. For unresectable disease, consider EBRT, RAI if avid, or systemic therapy. [PAP-9, FOLL-8, ONC-8]
For isolated distant metastases, consider local therapies (surgery, EBRT, ablation) if feasible. For widespread disease, systemic therapy. [PAP-10, FOLL-9, ONC-9]

prognosis.overall: Differentiated thyroid carcinomas usually have an excellent prognosis with 10-year survival rates exceeding 90% to 95%. In contrast, anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC) is almost uniformly lethal with a median survival of approximately 5 months and a 1-year survival rate of approximately 20%. Because differentiated thyroid carcinomas represent more than 95% of all cases, most thyroid carcinoma deaths are from papillary, follicular, and oncocytic carcinomas. In 2024, approximately 2,170 cancer deaths were estimated to occur among persons with thyroid carcinoma in the United States.

by_stage.stage: Differentiated carcinoma, all patients under age 55
by_stage.five_yr_survival: Excellent; patients classified as stage I or stage II under AJCC 8th edition, even those with distant metastases
by_stage.context: AJCC 8th edition staging classifies all patients under age 55 as stage I or II. Age is the most important prognostic variable for thyroid cancer mortality, with a cutoff of 55 years predicting the presence of distant metastasis. Age at diagnosis significantly influences outcomes: patients under age 40 have 5- and 10-year disease-specific survival rates of approximately 95% and 75%, respectively.

by_stage.stage: Differentiated carcinoma, patients 55 years and older, Stage I
by_stage.context: Stage I includes T1 N0/NX M0 and T2 N0/NX M0 for patients 55 years and older. Mortality rates are lower for younger individuals assigned female at birth.

by_stage.stage: Differentiated carcinoma, patients 55 years and older, Stage III-IV
by_stage.context: Stage III includes T4a disease. Stage IVA includes T4b. Stage IVB includes any T, any N, M1. Prognosis worsens with older age, advanced tumor stage, and larger tumor size.

Sporadic MTC typically presents in the fifth or sixth decade of life. The prognosis depends on the extent of disease at the time of initial surgery. Patients with undetectable postoperative calcitonin have excellent outcomes.
Stage IV includes local extension (T4a) or nodal spread (N1b) or distant metastases (M1). Rapid calcitonin and CEA doubling times are predictive of more aggressive disease.

ATC is almost uniformly fatal. Mean age at diagnosis is approximately 71 years. Patients with disease confined to the neck at diagnosis have a mean survival of 8 months compared with 3 months if disease extends beyond the neck.

prognostic_factors: Age at diagnosis (most important prognostic variable for thyroid cancer mortality)
Tumor stage and size (linear relationship between tumor size and recurrence or cancer-specific mortality)
Histologic subtype (aggressive variants including tall-cell, columnar-cell, hobnail, and diffuse sclerosing have worse prognosis)
Vascular invasion (associated with more aggressive disease and higher recurrence)
Extrathyroidal extension (recurrence rates two times higher with locally invasive tumors)
Lymph node metastases (especially associated with worse outcomes in older patients)
Distant metastases (principal cause of death from differentiated thyroid carcinomas)
BRAF V600E mutation status (when combined with TERT promoter mutation, associated with poor prognosis)
PD-L1 expression (associated with lower disease-free survival; HR 3.37; 95% CI 2.54–4.48; P < .00001)
Sex (risk of death from cancer approximately twice as high in individuals assigned male at birth)
RET codon status in medullary carcinoma (exon 16/M918T mutations associated with more aggressive disease)
Calcitonin/CEA doubling times in medullary carcinoma
Differentiation status of the tumor (poorly differentiated and anaplastic have markedly worse outcomes)
prognosis.prognostic_context: Although many factors influence the outcome for patients with papillary and follicular carcinomas, patient age at the time of initial therapy and tumor stage are important. Age is the most important prognostic variable for thyroid cancer mortality. Children typically present with more advanced disease and have more tumor recurrences after therapy than adults, yet their prognosis for survival is good. The disparity between cancer-related mortality and the frequency of tumor recurrence probably accounts for most of the disagreements among clinicians concerning optimal treatment. Several staging and clinical prognostic scoring strategies use patient age >40 years as a major feature to identify cancer mortality risk, including EORTC, TNM 7th edition, AMES, and AGES. With incrementally worsening MACIS scores of less than 6, 6 to 6.99, 7 to 7.99, and 8+, the 20-year survival rates are 99%, 89%, 56%, and 24%, respectively.

disease_related.management: Airway management is a paramount concern in anaplastic thyroid carcinoma. Tracheostomy is often a morbid and temporary treatment that may not be the option a patient would choose. Avoid prophylactic tracheostomy. Discuss palliative care options including airway management and IV steroids at the outset of diagnosis. Goals of therapy must be established early.

Many patients with advanced MTC have diarrhea and flushing because the tumor can secrete calcitonin and sometimes other hormonally active peptides. Treatment with somatostatin analogs (eg, octreotide, lanreotide) may be useful in patients with these symptoms.

disease_related.complication: Symptoms of upper aerodigestive tract compression or invasion (medullary carcinoma)
disease_related.management: Symptoms reported by up to 15% of patients with sporadic MTC. Surgical resection is the main treatment for MTC. In the presence of grossly invasive disease, more extended procedures with resection of involved neck structures may be appropriate.

chemotherapy: Doxorubicin: generally minimal efficacy in patients with metastatic differentiated thyroid disease
Cytotoxic systemic chemotherapy has been shown to have minimal efficacy, although most studies were small and underpowered
Single-agent paclitaxel may benefit some patients with newly diagnosed ATC; increased survival reported in patients with stage IVB disease
Dacarbazine-based chemotherapy regimens available for MTC
Treatment with anthracyclines and taxanes is generally not very effective for advanced anaplastic disease
immunotherapy: Ipilimumab + nivolumab: option for oncocytic carcinoma only
Pembrolizumab: option for TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) or MSI-H/dMMR tumors
Lenvatinib + pembrolizumab: option for anaplastic carcinoma
Immune checkpoint inhibitor-related toxicities require specific management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities
Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab
targeted_therapy: Kinase inhibitors may cause severe or fatal side effects including bleeding, hypertension, stroke, and liver toxicity
Lenvatinib: six treatment-related deaths occurred in the lenvatinib group in the SELECT trial
Sorafenib: one treatment-related death occurred in the sorafenib group; hand-foot syndrome common and may require dose adjustments
Vandetanib: cardiac toxicity involving prolongation of the QTc interval; restricted distribution through REMS program
Cabozantinib: rare adverse events include severe bleeding and gastrointestinal perforations or fistulas; severe hemorrhage is a contraindication
Dabrafenib/trametinib: common adverse events include fatigue (38%), pyrexia (37%), and nausea (35%)
Kinase inhibitors should be used with caution in patients with untreated CNS metastases due to bleeding risk
Optimal management of kinase inhibitor side effects is essential; dose modification may be required
surgery: Hypoparathyroidism: most common significant complication of thyroidectomy; transient clinical hypoparathyroidism is common in adults and children undergoing total thyroidectomy; reported hypocalcemia in 5.4% immediately after total thyroidectomy, persisting in only 0.5% at 1 year
Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury: reported incidence of 3.4% for long-term injury
Superior laryngeal nerve injury: under-reported and negatively impacts voice projection and high pitch range

Complications occur at lower rate when experienced surgeons perform thyroidectomies; surgeons performing >100 thyroidectomies/year had lowest complication rate (4.3%) versus those performing <10/year (four times as many complications)
Completion thyroidectomy complication rate is similar to that of total thyroidectomy
Tracheostomy in ATC: often morbid and temporary; avoid prophylactic tracheostomy
radiation: Radioactive iodine therapy risks: salivary gland dysfunction, lacrimal gland dysfunction, transient gonadal dysfunction, secondary primary malignancies (including leukemia and salivary gland malignancies in low-risk thyroid cancer patients receiving adjuvant RAI)
Nasolacrimal duct stenosis may occur with RAI
Diffuse lung metastases: maximum 48-hour whole-body dose should not exceed approximately 80 mCi to avoid pulmonary fibrosis
Bone marrow retention maximum should not exceed approximately 120 mCi at 48 hours
External beam radiation therapy: consider dental, speech and swallowing, and nutrition evaluation prior to RT
Patients with high (>1000 mCi) cumulative lifetime administered activities should be monitored for myelosuppression and potential long-term toxicities
RAI contraindicated in pregnancy due to risk of fetal hypothyroidism, malformation, and fetal demise

supportive_care.overview: The NCCN Guidelines for Thyroid Carcinoma do not provide a comprehensive supportive care section. The source document addresses calcium and vitamin D supplementation for patients with chronically suppressed TSH, and acknowledges the need for nutritional evaluation prior to radiation therapy.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Not specifically addressed in the source document. Nutritional evaluation is recommended prior to radiation therapy for thyroid carcinoma. Enteral nutrition may be useful for some patients with difficulty swallowing, particularly in anaplastic thyroid carcinoma.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not addressed in the source document.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not addressed in the source document.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not addressed in the source document.
supportive_care.pain_management: Not specifically addressed in the source document.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: The source document references the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health, with a reference to the NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A).
supportive_care.dental_care: Dental evaluation is recommended prior to radiation therapy for thyroid carcinoma. Patients whose TSH levels are chronically suppressed should be counseled to ensure adequate daily intake of elemental calcium and vitamin D (based on age and sex).

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After lobectomy for differentiated thyroid carcinoma, disease monitoring includes physical examination, TSH measurement, and neck ultrasound at 6-12 months. A one-time Tg and TgAb measurement may be considered. After total thyroidectomy without RAI, monitoring includes physical examination, TSH, Tg and TgAb measurement at 6-12 weeks, and neck ultrasound at 6-12 months. After total thyroidectomy with RAI, monitoring includes physical examination, neck ultrasound at 6-12 months, TSH, and Tg and TgAb measurement. If NED, physical examination, TSH, annual Tg and TgAb measurement if stable, and neck ultrasound every 1-3 years for 5-8 years, then discontinue. After 10-15 years of no recurrence for patients at low risk, no further monitoring for thyroid cancer is indicated. For medullary carcinoma, postoperative calcitonin and CEA should be measured 2-3 months postoperatively.
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Serum Tg determinations, neck ultrasound, and whole body iodine-131 imaging detect recurrent or residual disease in most patients who have undergone total thyroid ablation. Tg measurement is the best means of detecting thyroid tissue, including carcinoma. About 85% of patients are considered to be low risk after surgery for papillary thyroid carcinoma.
late_effects_screening: Bone mineral density monitoring for patients on long-term TSH-suppressive levothyroxine therapy, particularly post-menopausal patients
Cardiac monitoring for tachyarrhythmias in elderly patients on TSH-suppressive doses of levothyroxine
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation counseling for patients with chronically suppressed TSH
Salivary and lacrimal gland function monitoring after RAI therapy
Screening for secondary malignancies in patients who received RAI therapy
Screening for pheochromocytoma (annual, MEN2B and MEN2A) and hyperparathyroidism (annual, MEN2A) in patients with hereditary MTC
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: About 75% of patients with differentiated thyroid carcinoma show tumor recurrences during the first 5 years following treatment, with the remaining recurrences occurring within 8 years after treatment. Nonpalpable, small-volume lymph node recurrences often show little evidence of disease progression over many years and do not appear to be associated with an increase in mortality. About 50% of distant metastases are present at the time of diagnosis. Distant metastases occur even more often in patients with oncocytic carcinoma (35%) and in those patients who are >40 years at diagnosis. Sites of reported distant metastases: lung (49%), bone (25%), both lung and bone (15%), and the central nervous system or other soft tissues (10%).

clinical_follow_up_schedule: After lobectomy: physical examination, TSH at 6-12 months, neck ultrasound at 6-12 months, consider one-time Tg and TgAb measurement
After total thyroidectomy without RAI: physical examination, TSH, Tg and TgAb at 6-12 weeks, neck ultrasound at 6-12 months
After total thyroidectomy with RAI: physical examination, neck ultrasound at 6-12 months, TSH, Tg and TgAb measurement
NED after total thyroidectomy with RAI: physical examination, TSH, Tg and TgAb annually if stable, neck ultrasound every 1-3 years for 5-8 years then discontinue
NED after total thyroidectomy without RAI: physical examination, TSH, Tg and TgAb measurement annually if stable, neck ultrasound as clinically indicated
After 10-15 years of no recurrence for patients at low risk: no further monitoring for thyroid cancer is indicated
Medullary carcinoma with undetectable calcitonin and normal CEA: annual serum calcitonin and CEA, consider neck ultrasound
Medullary carcinoma with detectable calcitonin or elevated CEA: serum calcitonin, CEA every 6-12 months, neck ultrasound
In select patients with higher risk (eg, N1 patients): obtain a Tg measurement and consider concomitant diagnostic RAI imaging
Active surveillance for low-risk papillary microcarcinoma (≤1 cm): neck ultrasound every 6 months for 1-2 years, then annually
imaging_strategy: Neck ultrasound including central and lateral cervical nodal compartments is the recommended principal imaging modality for differentiated thyroid carcinoma
CT or MRI with contrast for locally advanced disease or vocal cord paresis
Chest CT for evaluation of pulmonary metastases (can be done without contrast)
FDG-PET/CT or FDG-PET/MRI as clinically indicated, particularly for RAI-refractory disease or when Tg is elevated with negative RAI imaging
Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT for medullary carcinoma (if available, consider bone scan and/or whole body MRI)
Contrast-enhanced MRI of neck, chest, and abdomen using a liver protocol for MTC when calcitonin is elevated
Whole body MRI in patients with very elevated calcitonin levels
Diagnostic whole body iodine-123 or iodine-131 imaging may be considered prior to RAI therapy
Post-treatment iodine-131 whole body imaging should be performed in all cases
Brain MRI with contrast prior to starting systemic therapy for advanced disease
PET scan is indicated for patients with a negative whole body scan who have suspected structural disease and/or elevated Tg
laboratory_monitoring: TSH measurement: for monitoring levothyroxine dose adjustment and TSH suppression adequacy
Thyroglobulin (Tg) measurement: best means of detecting thyroid tissue including carcinoma; use same laboratory and same assay for consistent results
TgAb (antithyroglobulin antibodies): measured in same serum sample as Tg because these antibodies invalidate serum Tg measurements; functional sensitivity ≤0.9 ng/mL for newer assays
Stimulated Tg no longer necessary with availability of ultrasensitive second-generation Tg immunometric assays
Postoperative unstimulated Tg measured 6-12 weeks after total thyroidectomy
Postoperative calcitonin measured 2-3 months after surgery for MTC
CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen) measured 2-3 months after surgery for MTC
If calcitonin is ≥150 pg/mL, consider CT or MRI of neck, chest, and liver
Rising Tg or new TgAb should prompt additional imaging
Interpretation of rising or new TgAb is assay dependent and best performed as radioimmunoassay with consistent assay for trends
Data are currently lacking for a specific Tg cutoff after lobectomy, but if very elevated in absence of other causes, consider further imaging
supportive_follow_up: Consider NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship for long-term follow-up
Counsel patients on adequate calcium and vitamin D intake if TSH is chronically suppressed
Screen for pheochromocytoma and hyperparathyroidism annually in patients with MEN2
Genetic counseling for patients with germline RET pathogenic variants

special_situations.situation: Hereditary medullary thyroid carcinoma - MEN2B (RET M918T mutation)
special_situations.key_message: Highest risk RET mutation; surgery recommended during the first year of life
special_situations.management: Total thyroidectomy during childhood or at diagnosis. Surgery is recommended during the first year of life for patients with M918T mutation. Therapeutic neck dissection guided by imaging. Consider prophylactic central neck dissection (level VI). Postoperative administration of levothyroxine to normalize TSH (not suppressive). Annual screening for pheochromocytoma.
special_situations.evidence: The M918T mutation is considered highest risk with MTC usually presenting at a younger age. Category 2A recommendation.

special_situations.situation: Hereditary medullary thyroid carcinoma - MEN2A with high-risk RET PV (codon 634 and A883F)
special_situations.key_message: High-risk mutations; surgery recommended before age 5
special_situations.management: Surgery before age 5 based on serum calcitonin levels. Total thyroidectomy and dissection of lymph node compartments based on ultrasound findings and preoperative serum calcitonin. For patients without primary hyperparathyroidism, surgery before age 5 based on serum calcitonin levels.
special_situations.evidence: Codon 634 and A883F mutations are considered high risk, with MTC usually presenting at a younger age. Category 2A recommendation.

special_situations.situation: Hereditary medullary thyroid carcinoma - MEN2A with moderate-risk RET PV
special_situations.key_message: Moderate risk; prophylactic thyroidectomy may be delayed if surveillance criteria met
special_situations.management: Prophylactic thyroidectomy may be delayed, provided annual basal calcitonin measurement is normal, annual ultrasound is unremarkable, and there is no history of aggressive MTC in the family.
special_situations.evidence: Most other RET PVs associated with MEN2A or FMTC are generally moderate risk. Surveillance may be appropriate per shared decision making. Category 2A recommendation.

special_situations.situation: Hereditary medullary thyroid carcinoma - low-risk RET PV (V804M)
special_situations.key_message: Low lifetime risk of MTC (~4%); surveillance may be a viable long-term strategy
special_situations.management: Surveillance may be a viable long-term strategy in the absence of a strong family history of MTC. Surveillance with annual serum calcitonin measurement and ultrasound. Total thyroidectomy may be considered.
special_situations.evidence: V804M mutation is common but carries a low lifetime risk of MTC (~4%). Category 2A recommendation.

special_situations.situation: Active surveillance for low-risk papillary thyroid microcarcinoma
special_situations.key_message: Low-quality evidence supports active surveillance as an appropriate management option for some patients with low-risk PTC ≤1 cm
special_situations.management: Neck ultrasound every 6 months for 1-2 years, then annually. If suspicious nodes present, evaluate prior to continuing surveillance. Transition to surgery for patient preference, new lymph node metastases, distant metastases, nerve/trachea/esophageal invasion, ETE, or tumor growth by 3 mm or 50% volume increase.
special_situations.evidence: Low-quality evidence from studies in Japan and elsewhere. Not recommended if: aggressive histologic subtypes on FNA, invasion of recurrent laryngeal nerve/trachea/esophagus, visible ETE, regional or distant metastases, tumor near posterior capsule, abutting trachea, patient unwilling to follow up, or lack of access to high-quality neck ultrasound. Category 2A recommendation.

special_situations.key_message: Defined by absence of RAI uptake, heterogeneous uptake, or disease progression despite RAI therapy; requires molecular testing and systemic therapy
special_situations.management: Molecular analysis to identify actionable somatic alterations including ALK, NTRK1/2/3, BRAF, and RET gene fusions. Brain imaging prior to starting systemic therapy. Preferred systemic therapy: lenvatinib or sorafenib. Consider clinical trials. Other options include cabozantinib, RET inhibitors, NTRK inhibitors, pembrolizumab for TMB-H/MSI-H/dMMR.
special_situations.evidence: RAI-refractory defined as: no RAI uptake on diagnostic scan; no RAI uptake on scan after therapy; heterogeneous uptake; disease progression despite RAI uptake; or progression despite cumulative iodine-131 activity >600 mCi. Lenvatinib: PFS 18.3 vs 3.6 months (HR 0.21; P < .001). Sorafenib: PFS 10.8 vs 5.8 months (HR 0.59; P < .0001).

special_situations.key_message: Targeted therapy with dabrafenib/trametinib is a preferred option; may enable surgical resection in some patients
special_situations.management: Biomarker testing should include BRAF IHC (faster turnaround) and molecular testing for BRAF, NTRK1/2/3, ALK, and RET. BRAF V600E-positive: preferred systemic therapy is dabrafenib/trametinib or binimetinib/encorafenib. Neoadjuvant dabrafenib/trametinib for resectable disease may be considered (category 2B). Complete surgical resection achieved in six patients with initially unresectable BRAF V600E-mutated ATC after neoadjuvant dabrafenib/trametinib; 1-year OS was 83%.
special_situations.evidence: Phase 2 trial: ORR 56% (95% CI 38.1%–72.1%), median PFS 6.7 months, median OS 14.5 months. FDA approved 2018.

special_situations.key_message: RAI should be avoided in pregnancy due to risk of fetal hypothyroidism, malformation, and fetal demise
special_situations.management: Surgery can be performed during the second trimester if indicated. Levothyroxine to maintain TSH in low-normal range. In selective cases, integrating care with reproductive endocrinology/oncofertility may be appropriate.
special_situations.evidence: RAI contraindicated in pregnancy. Documentation of negative pregnancy test required before RAI administration.

special_situations.key_message: Rising Tg or TgAb with negative structural imaging should prompt additional workup but systemic therapy is not typically indicated without structural disease
special_situations.management: Consider additional imaging (neck CT/MRI with contrast or chest/abdomen CT), FDG-PET, or RAI imaging. Monitor with TSH, Tg, TgAb measurements, and neck ultrasound as clinically indicated. Systemic therapy generally not indicated on basis of abnormal markers alone in absence of radiographically evident structural disease.
special_situations.evidence: Rising biomarkers in the absence of structural disease progression are not an indication for treatment with systemic therapy.

special_situations.key_message: Differentiated thyroid carcinomas in children typically present with more advanced disease and higher recurrence rates but have good long-term survival
special_situations.management: Children typically present with more advanced disease and have more tumor recurrences after therapy than adults, yet their prognosis for survival is good (90% at 20 years). Weight-based dose adjustment for RAI therapy. Referral to high-volume center for prophylactic thyroidectomy in very young children.
special_situations.evidence: Standardized mortality ratio 8-fold higher than predicted in children, but long-term survival is favorable.

clinical_pearls: Age at diagnosis is the most important prognostic variable for thyroid cancer mortality; the AJCC 8th edition uses age 55 as the cutoff for staging rather than the previous 45 years.

NIFTP (noninvasive follicular thyroid neoplasm with papillary-like nuclear features), formerly known as encapsulated follicular variant of PTC, has been reclassified and only lobectomy is needed; it has a low risk for adverse outcomes.
Microscopic extrathyroidal extension alone likely does not warrant RAI therapy, distinguishing it from gross ETE which is an indication for RAI.

After 10-15 years of no recurrence for patients at low risk, no further monitoring for thyroid cancer is indicated, reflecting the excellent long-term prognosis of low-risk differentiated thyroid carcinoma.

BRAF V600E mutation occurs in approximately 45% of patients with papillary carcinoma; while generally not considered associated with poor prognosis on its own, when combined with TERT promoter mutation it is associated with worse outcomes.
Prophylactic central neck dissection is not indicated in most papillary thyroid cancers based on results of three randomized controlled trials showing no clear benefit.
Selpercatinib has shown superior PFS compared to cabozantinib or vandetanib as first-line therapy for RET-mutant medullary thyroid cancer (median PFS not reached vs 16.8 months; HR 0.28; P < .001).

RAI-refractory disease requires specific diagnostic criteria including absence of uptake on scans, heterogeneous uptake, or disease progression despite RAI therapy, and is defined by an international consensus statement.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Thyroid Carcinoma, Version 2.2026
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive guidelines covering papillary, follicular, oncocytic, medullary, and anaplastic thyroid carcinoma. Updated June 2, 2026. Includes principles of TSH suppression, systemic therapy, radiation and radioactive iodine therapy, active surveillance, and cancer risk assessment.

guidelines_resources.title: 2015 American Thyroid Association Management Guidelines for Adult Patients with Thyroid Nodules and Differentiated Thyroid Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive guidelines on thyroid nodule evaluation and differentiated thyroid cancer management. Referenced for ultrasound surveillance, nodule management, and treatment algorithms.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition (2017)
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides TNM staging definitions for differentiated thyroid carcinoma, medullary carcinoma, and anaplastic carcinoma. The 8th edition changed the age cutoff from 45 to 55 years for staging.

guidelines_resources.title: Protocol for the Examination of Specimens From Patients With Carcinomas of the Thyroid Gland
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides standardized pathology reporting for thyroid carcinomas including definitions of vascular invasion and other terms.

guidelines_resources.title: Revised American Thyroid Association Guidelines for the Management of Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma (2015)
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive guidelines specific to medullary thyroid carcinoma management including RET testing, surgical approach, and surveillance.

guidelines_resources.title: 2021 American Thyroid Association Guidelines for Management of Patients with Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Guidelines specific to anaplastic thyroid cancer management including multimodality therapy approaches.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A)
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for psychosocial support including the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for principles of cancer risk assessment and counseling for hereditary thyroid cancer syndromes including Cowden syndrome/PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome.

key_trials.full_name: Study of E7080, a Multiple Receptor Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor, in Differentiated Cancer of the Thyroid
key_trials.population: Radioiodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer (papillary, follicular, or Hürthle cell)
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: 18.3 months vs 3.6 months (HR, 0.21; 99% CI, 0.14–0.31; P < .001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Six treatment-related deaths occurred in the lenvatinib group; older patients (≥65 years) treated with lenvatinib had longer median OS compared to placebo (HR, 0.27; 95% CI 0.31–0.91; P = .20); benefit maintained regardless of age (cut-off 65 years)
key_trials.practice_change: Lenvatinib became preferred systemic therapy option for RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer (Category 1)

key_trials.full_name: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Phase 3 Trial Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Sorafenib in Patients with Locally Advanced or Metastatic Radioactive Iodine-Refractory Differentiated Thyroid Cancer
key_trials.population: Radioiodine-refractory, locally advanced or metastatic differentiated thyroid cancer
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: 10.8 months vs 5.8 months (HR, 0.59; 95% CI, 0.45–0.76; P < .0001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: One treatment-related death in sorafenib group; hand-foot syndrome common requiring dose adjustments
key_trials.practice_change: Sorafenib became a category 1 option for RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer

key_trials.full_name: Cabozantinib for Radioiodine-Refractory Differentiated Thyroid Cancer
key_trials.population: Radioiodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer that progressed during or after treatment with one or two VEGFR TKIs (including lenvatinib and/or sorafenib)
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: not reached in cabozantinib arm vs 1.9 months in placebo arm (HR, 0.22; 99% CI, 0.13–0.36; P < .0001); updated: 11.0 vs 1.9 months (HR, 0.22; 95% CI, 0.15–0.32; P < .0001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR: 11.0% vs 0% (P = .0003); PFS benefit maintained regardless of histology and previous VEGFR TKI treatment
key_trials.practice_change: Cabozantinib became category 1 option for papillary carcinoma and category 2A for follicular/oncocytic carcinoma after lenvatinib and/or sorafenib progression

key_trials.full_name: A Randomized, Double-Blind Phase III Trial of Vandetanib in Patients with Locally Advanced or Metastatic Medullary Thyroid Cancer
key_trials.population: Unresectable, locally advanced, or metastatic medullary thyroid cancer
key_trials.key_result: PFS improved (HR, 0.46; 95% CI, 0.31–0.69; P < .001); OS data not yet available
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Post-hoc subgroup of symptomatic/progressive disease: PFS HR 0.43 (95% CI 0.28–0.64; P < .001); ORR 37% vs 2% (P < .001)
key_trials.practice_change: Vandetanib became category 1 preferred option for recurrent/persistent MTC; access restricted through REMS program

key_trials.full_name: A Randomized Phase III Trial of Cabozantinib in Patients with Progressive, Metastatic Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Locally advanced or metastatic medullary thyroid cancer
key_trials.key_result: PFS: 11.2 vs 4.0 months (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.19–0.40; P < .001); median OS 26.6 vs 21.1 months (stratified HR, 0.85; 95% CI 0.64–1.12; P = .24, not statistically significant)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 11.0% vs 0%; may have greater clinical benefit for RET M918T or RAS mutations
key_trials.practice_change: Cabozantinib became category 1 preferred option for recurrent/persistent MTC

key_trials.full_name: Phase 3 Trial of Selpercatinib in Advanced RET-Mutant Medullary Thyroid Cancer
key_trials.population: Progressive RET-mutant medullary thyroid cancer, first-line treatment
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 69.4% (95% CI 62.4%–75.8%) vs 38.8% (95% CI 29.1%–49.2%); median treatment failure-free survival not reached vs 13.9 months (HR 0.25; 95% CI 0.15–0.42; P < .001)
key_trials.practice_change: Selpercatinib became category 1 preferred option for RET-mutant MTC; also FDA approved for pediatric patients ≥2 years of age

key_trials.full_name: Dabrafenib and Trametinib Treatment in Patients with Locally Advanced or Metastatic BRAF V600-Mutant Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer
key_trials.population: Locally advanced or metastatic BRAF V600E-mutant anaplastic thyroid cancer
key_trials.intervention: Dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily + trametinib 2 mg once daily
key_trials.key_result: ORR: 56% (95% CI, 38.1%–72.1%), including 3 complete responses; median PFS 6.7 months; median OS 14.5 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: 12-month OS rate 43.2%; 12-month PFS rate 51.7%; 12-month duration of response 50%; well tolerated
key_trials.practice_change: FDA approved 2018 for BRAF V600E-mutated ATC; became preferred systemic therapy option

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab in Patients with Advanced Solid Tumors with High Tumor Mutational Burden
key_trials.population: Advanced solid tumors including thyroid cancer; TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb)
key_trials.key_result: For the whole sample: ORR 29% (95% CI 21%–39%); two patients with thyroid cancer included
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3-5 treatment-related adverse events in 15% of patients
key_trials.practice_change: Pembrolizumab became an option for TMB-H differentiated thyroid cancer
